content.text: What it is

Living documentation from BDD scenarios is the practice of automatically generating and publishing human-readable feature documentation from the Gherkin scenarios in the test suite, with current pass/fail status attached. Unlike static product documentation that describes what the feature was designed to do, living documentation describes what the feature currently does as verified by automated tests. Every time the test suite runs, the documentation is regenerated: a scenario that passes is marked as working; a scenario that fails is marked as broken; a scenario that is skipped is marked as pending.

For agentic AI features, living documentation adds an additional layer of information: the eval quality score trend alongside the BDD pass/fail status. A feature whose BDD scenarios pass but whose eval quality score is declining over time tells a story that static documentation cannot — the feature meets its minimum acceptance criteria but is degrading in quality. Living documentation surfaces this signal to product managers, customer success teams, and engineering leadership without requiring them to read test run logs.
Why it matters

Product documentation becomes stale as soon as the feature changes. In an agentic AI context, features change frequently: the system prompt is updated, a new tool is added, a refusal condition is tightened, or the underlying model version changes. Each of these changes can alter the feature's observable behaviour. If the documentation is not updated alongside the feature, stakeholders make decisions based on a description of how the feature used to work rather than how it currently works.

Living documentation solves this by making the test suite the single source of truth for feature behaviour. Engineers maintain the Gherkin scenarios as they change the feature, and the documentation is generated automatically. The documentation never diverges from the tested behaviour because it is generated from the tests themselves. For regulated industries where feature behaviour must be auditable, living documentation provides a timestamped record of what the feature was doing at any point in time.
Benefits
Documentation is always current: it is generated from the test suite, so it reflects the tested behaviour of the current deployed version
Stakeholders can read feature behaviour in plain English from the Gherkin scenarios without accessing the codebase
Pass/fail status embedded in the documentation alerts non-technical readers to known feature gaps without requiring them to read CI logs
Provides an auditable record of feature behaviour over time for compliance and regulatory purposes
Encourages engineers to write better Gherkin because they know the scenarios will be read by a broad audience
Trade-offs and limitations

Living documentation requires well-written Gherkin: if the scenarios are written in technical terms or describe implementation rather than behaviour, the generated documentation is unreadable by non-technical stakeholders

Pass/fail status can be misleading for AI features: a passing BDD scenario using a VCR cassette confirms structural correctness, not quality; the documentation must clarify which scenarios use real model calls
Publishing living documentation externally or to senior stakeholders creates pressure not to have failing scenarios, which can incentivise marking failing scenarios as pending to hide problems
The documentation publishing pipeline adds infrastructure complexity: a tool (Allure, Cucumber Reports, Serenity) must generate the HTML, and a deployment step must publish it to an accessible URL
How it works

When the BDD test suite runs in CI, the test runner (pytest-bdd, Cucumber, Behave) outputs a structured result file — typically JSON or XML — that includes each scenario's title, steps, and pass/fail/skip status. A report generation tool reads this output and produces an HTML document organised by feature. The HTML document renders the Gherkin scenarios in a readable format, highlights failing scenarios, and includes metadata such as the run time, the commit SHA, and the feature tag set.

For agentic AI features, the living documentation is enriched with eval quality data. After the nightly eval suite run, the quality dashboard API is queried for the current score for each feature. The documentation publishing step reads both the BDD results and the eval quality scores and renders them together: each feature section shows the BDD scenario pass/fail grid alongside a quality score summary and a trend arrow (improving, stable, declining) derived from the last four eval runs.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Living documentation generation pipeline: BDD suite and eval suite produce result files, a report generator merges them into published living docs with pass/fail and quality trend

content.text: The BDD suite produces structured JSON/XML on each CI run. The eval suite produces per-feature quality scores on nightly runs. The report generator reads both, merges them by feature name, and publishes the combined HTML to an internal documentation portal. Feature panels show the scenario pass/fail grid from the BDD results and the quality trend from the eval history.
When to use it

Introduce living documentation when the team has a working BDD test suite and stakeholders are struggling to understand the current state of agentic AI features. It is particularly valuable in organisations where product managers and customer success teams need to understand which AI features are working as specified without being able to read test code. The investment in the publishing pipeline pays off most when the feature set is large enough that static documentation maintenance is already failing — typically when more than five agentic AI features are in production simultaneously.
How to implement
Ensure all Gherkin scenarios are written in business-readable language — review them against the criterion: could a product manager read this and explain it to a customer?
Add allure-pytest (or Serenity BDD for Cucumber) as a test dependency; configure the test runner to produce JSON output alongside the normal test results
Add a post-test CI step that runs the report generator against the JSON output to produce the HTML report
Add a publishing step that uploads the HTML report to the internal documentation portal (GitHub Pages, Confluence, a static site host)
For the eval quality enrichment: add a step after the nightly eval run that fetches per-feature scores and writes them as a sidecar JSON file alongside the BDD results
Extend the report generator to read the sidecar quality JSON and render a quality trend summary beneath each feature's scenario list
Add a Slack or email notification that announces when the living docs are updated, so stakeholders know when to look at the latest version
Examples

# .github/workflows/living-docs.yml
# Generates and publishes living documentation after the BDD test suite

name: Living Documentation

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'  # 6am UTC daily (after nightly eval suite)

jobs:
  bdd-and-docs:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pytest pytest-bdd allure-pytest

      - name: Run BDD suite (stub path)
        run: |
          pytest tests/bdd/ -m stub \
            --alluredir=allure-results \
            --tb=short \
            -v

      - name: Fetch eval quality scores
        env:
          EVAL_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.EVAL_API_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          python scripts/fetch_eval_scores.py \
            --output allure-results/eval-quality.json

      - name: Generate Allure HTML report
        run: |
          allure generate allure-results \
            --clean \
            --output allure-report

      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        uses: peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v4
        with:
          github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          publish_dir: allure-report
          destination_dir: living-docs

# scripts/fetch_eval_scores.py
# Fetches per-feature eval quality scores and writes them for Allure enrichment

import json
import os
import argparse
import requests

def fetch_scores(output_path: str) -> None:
    api_base = os.environ['EVAL_API_BASE']
    token = os.environ['EVAL_API_TOKEN']

    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'}
    response = requests.get(
        f'{api_base}/api/v1/features/scores/latest',
        headers=headers,
        timeout=30
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    scores = response.json()  # {'feature_id': {'score': 0.87, 'trend': 'declining', 'runs': 4}}

    with open(output_path, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(scores, f, indent=2)

    print(f'Wrote eval quality scores for {len(scores)} features to {output_path}')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--output', required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    fetch_scores(args.output)
Real-world example

A fintech company with seven agentic AI features in production was spending an hour per sprint answering customer success questions about which features worked as specified. Customer success had no way to check the current state of any feature without asking an engineer. After introducing living documentation published to an internal web page, the customer success team bookmarked the documentation URL and checked it themselves before escalating to engineering. The first month after launch, engineering received 70% fewer 'is this feature still broken?' questions, and customer success was able to proactively identify two features whose eval quality scores had been declining for three weeks before anyone noticed.
Common mistakes
Publishing living documentation from @live scenarios: live-model BDD runs are non-deterministic; use only @stub (VCR cassette) results for the published pass/fail status
Showing passing status without clarifying that it reflects cassette replay, not live model quality: add a visible banner explaining the two quality signals
Skipping scenarios instead of marking them failed to keep the published documentation clean: failing scenarios are the most important information; never hide them
Not regenerating living docs on every BDD run: stale documentation is misleading; regenerate and republish on every commit to main and on every nightly run
Best practices
Add a 'last updated' timestamp and commit SHA to every page so readers know whether they are looking at the current build's documentation
Add a prominent banner on each feature panel explaining the difference between BDD structural pass/fail (cassette) and eval quality score (live model)
Group scenarios by tag: present @safety scenarios in a dedicated section so safety reviewers can quickly find and monitor the safety coverage
Link the Gherkin scenario in the living docs back to the relevant source file in the repository; engineers can jump from the documentation to the specification with one click
Send a weekly digest email with a summary of any feature whose eval quality trend changed from stable to declining, so stakeholders are proactively informed rather than reactive
Performance and scalability

The report generation step (Allure HTML generation) typically takes 15–30 seconds for a suite with up to 200 scenarios. The publishing step (GitHub Pages deployment or S3 upload) takes an additional 10–20 seconds. For organisations with large feature sets (50+ features, 1000+ scenarios), consider generating the report incrementally by feature rather than regenerating the entire report on every CI run. Only features whose test results changed need to be regenerated.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
allure-pytest
Report generation for Python BDD
Produces rich HTML with scenario-level detail; integrates with pytest-bdd
Serenity BDD
Living documentation for Cucumber / JVM
Purpose-built for living documentation from Gherkin; strong narrative reporting
Cucumber Reports
Cloud-hosted living docs
Hosted service; auto-publishes from CI; multi-project dashboard
GitHub Pages / Confluence
Documentation hosting
Host the generated HTML internally; GitHub Pages free for private repos on paid plans
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Living documentation (this topic)
Always current; shows pass/fail; non-technical stakeholders can read
Requires well-written Gherkin; publishing pipeline overhead
Static product documentation
Full narrative control; easy to write
Becomes stale immediately; no pass/fail status; diverges from implementation
Test CI dashboard (raw)
Always current; detailed failure information
Not readable by non-technical stakeholders; shows test names not behaviour descriptions
Security and reliability

The living documentation portal is accessible to internal stakeholders. Treat it as an internal document, not a public page. The Gherkin scenarios may contain sample inputs (prompts, edge cases, refusal triggers) that reveal the feature's capability boundaries and safety conditions. Hosting this information publicly would allow adversaries to design prompt injection attempts targeting the specific refusal conditions the agent uses. Apply the same access controls to the documentation portal as to the feature's product specification.
Structuring the published documentation portal

A living documentation portal for multiple agentic AI features should be organised by product area or user journey, not by test suite file structure. Non-technical stakeholders think in terms of 'the expense categorisation feature' or 'the contract review feature', not in terms of 'the testing-validation-phase BDD suite'. The index page should list each feature by its product name with a health badge (all passing / some failing / no coverage) so stakeholders can immediately identify which features need attention.

Each feature detail page should present: the feature description in plain English (from the Gherkin Feature block), the Background steps (shared preconditions), the scenario list grouped by scenario type (happy path, edge case, safety/refusal), the current pass/fail status for each scenario, the eval quality score and trend arrow, and the date of the last update. Do not include step definitions or test implementation details — those belong in the engineering wiki, not the living documentation.
Index page: list features by product name with health badge; sort by number of failing scenarios descending
Feature detail page: feature description, scenario list, pass/fail grid, eval quality summary, last-updated timestamp
Safety section: dedicated tab on each feature page showing only @safety-tagged scenarios with their pass/fail status
Change history: link to the git blame or changelog for the Gherkin feature file so readers can see when each scenario was added or modified
Representing AI quality signals in the documentation

The dual signal for agentic AI features — BDD structural pass/fail and eval quality score — must be presented carefully to avoid misinterpretation. A feature with all BDD scenarios passing and a declining eval quality score is not 'fine'; it is degrading. The documentation must make this distinction visible without requiring the reader to understand the technical difference between cassette replay and live model evaluation.
Use two distinct visual indicators: a green/red badge for BDD structural status, and a score number with an up/down trend arrow for eval quality — never merge them into a single status

Include a plain-English explanation on each page: 'Structural checks: verified the feature works as specified using recorded responses. Quality score: measured against live model on nightly evaluation.'
Highlight the trend direction more prominently than the absolute score: a declining score at 0.87 is more concerning than a stable score at 0.79
Show the last four eval scores on a mini sparkline chart so readers can immediately see whether a declining trend started recently or has been ongoing for weeks
Keeping Gherkin readable for non-technical audiences

The readability of living documentation is entirely dependent on the quality of the Gherkin scenarios it is generated from. Gherkin written in technical terms — referencing API endpoints, model parameters, or test infrastructure — will produce documentation that non-technical stakeholders cannot understand. Before enabling living documentation, conduct a Gherkin readability review with a product manager and a customer success representative who were not involved in writing the scenarios: if they cannot understand a scenario, the scenario must be rewritten.
Write scenario titles as complete sentences describing the business outcome: 'Categorise a travel expense with a clear description' not 'happy_path_travel'
Use domain language in step text: 'when the expense is submitted for categorisation' not 'when POST /api/v1/expenses is called'
Avoid implementation details in Then steps: 'the response contains a category and a confidence level' not 'the JSON response contains category_id and confidence_score fields'
Run a quarterly readability check: ask a team member who did not write the scenarios to explain each scenario to a colleague without reading from the screen

Teams that adopt living documentation report a significant reduction in the number of specification-implementation gaps discovered at UAT time. When stakeholders can read the current tested behaviour at any point during the sprint, they raise concerns earlier — during development rather than at the UAT gate. The feedback loop tightens from weeks to days. For agentic AI features where acceptable behaviour involves judgement calls (confidence thresholds, clarification conditions, refusal boundaries), this early stakeholder visibility is particularly valuable because it allows product owners to recalibrate the acceptance criteria before the engineering work is complete.

The investment in living documentation infrastructure pays dividends beyond the team that builds it. When a new product manager joins, they can read the living documentation to understand the current state of every AI feature without onboarding calls with engineers. When a security audit requires evidence of what the AI system does in specific scenarios, the living documentation provides timestamped proof. When a customer escalation requires understanding whether a specific behaviour is 'by design', the living documentation provides the answer within minutes rather than hours of investigation.
Handling breaking changes in the documentation pipeline

When a major change to the Gherkin feature file is approved under the design change control process — a new capability is added, a refusal condition is changed, a Scenario Outline gains new example rows — the living documentation must reflect the change in the next published version without creating confusing interim states. The safest approach is to update the Gherkin file and the VCR cassettes in the same PR and run the full documentation generation before merging, so the published documentation transitions atomically from the old specification to the new one.

Never delete old Gherkin scenarios without first marking them @deprecated in a separate PR; allow one full publish cycle before removing them so stakeholders see the deprecation notice before the scenario disappears

When a new capability is added, the new scenarios will appear with a pending status until the cassettes are recorded; add an explanatory note in the feature's Background block during this interim period
Version the living documentation alongside the major version of the feature: maintain a version history URL so stakeholders can compare what the feature did before and after a major change
Key takeaways
Living documentation generates human-readable feature documentation automatically from the Gherkin test suite with current pass/fail status, eliminating documentation drift
For agentic AI features, enrich the living docs with eval quality scores and trend arrows alongside the BDD pass/fail grid
Publish only @stub (cassette) BDD results as the pass/fail signal; clearly label the distinction from live-model quality scores
Never hide failing scenarios in the published documentation — failing scenarios are the most valuable signal for non-technical stakeholders
Apply internal access controls to the living docs portal; the scenario examples reveal the feature's safety conditions and capability boundaries
